sections.paragraph:
(segol. form, like מָוֶת, אָרֶץ), subst. distance, remoteness, always with ה parag. הָ֫לְאָה (Milêl, and on that account without Metheg), adverb far off, farther.

(1) Used of space, Gen 19:9, נֶּשׁ־הָלְאָה “go farther back,” begone; LXX. ἀπόστα ἐκεῖ: Vulg. recede illuc (see my Commentary on Isa 49:20). According to others, “come nearer,” which is incorrect, and unsuitable to the sense. 1Sa 10:3, מִשָּׁם וָהָלְאָח “(and thou shalt go) thence farther;” 1Sa 20:22, מִמְּךָ וָהָלְאָה “from thee farther,” i.e. beyond thee; verse 37 1Sa 20:37. (In opposition to מִמְּךָ וָהֵנָּה on this side of thee.) Num 32:19 Isa 18:2, עַם נֹורָא מִן־הוּא וָהָלְאָה “a people terrible and farther off than it;” verse Isa 18:7. מֵהָלְאָה לְ “father off than,” as Amo 5:27, מֵהָלְאָה לְדַמֶּשֶׂק “beyond, farther off than Damascus.”

(2) Used of time, 1Sa 18:9, מֵהַיֹּום הַהוּא וָהָלְאָה “from that day and onward;” Lev 22:27. (Syr. ܗܰܠ, ܗܰܠ id., ܠܗܰܠ ܡܶܢ beyond, Ch. לְהַלָּא, לְהַלָּא and לְהַלָּן, which belong to a root Med. Gem., see under הָלָא .) [In Thes. Gesenius thus derives the word, “probably for הַ֫לָּה from הַל (which see, No. 1).”]
